name: Polonium
name_origin: Named by Marie Sklodowska-Curie in 1898 after her native country, Poland (Latin 'Polonia'), to draw attention to Poland's lack of independence at the time.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Polonium is extremely rare in Earth's crust, on the order of 10^-10 ppm. It occurs only as short-lived intermediates in the natural uranium-238 and thorium-232 decay chains; 210Po, the most familiar isotope, is found in trace amounts in uranium ores. Practical quantities are produced artificially by neutron irradiation of bismuth-209 in nuclear reactors.
states: trace in uranium and thorium ores as decay-chain intermediate
produced by neutron irradiation of 209Bi in reactors
trace in tobacco leaves (radiological concern in cigarettes)
trace in soils and the atmosphere from Rn-222 progeny

compounds.name: Polonium dioxide
compounds.summary: A pale yellow tetragonal solid that is the most stable oxide of polonium and resembles SeO2 and TeO2 in structure.

compounds.name: Polonium hydride (polane)
compounds.summary: An unstable, volatile liquid analog of H2O, H2S, H2Se, and H2Te that is exceedingly difficult to study because of intense alpha radiolysis.

compounds.name: Polonium tetrachloride
compounds.summary: A bright yellow crystalline solid used as a starting material for polonium chemistry; sublimes readily under vacuum.

compounds.name: Polonium dichloride
compounds.summary: A dark ruby-red solid representing the +2 oxidation state, more stable in solid form than as Po(II) cation in solution.

compounds.name: Sodium polonite
compounds.summary: A polonate(IV) salt formed by reaction of polonium oxide with strong base, illustrating polonium's chalcogen-like chemistry.

uses: Static eliminators in industrial settings (alpha radiation ionizes air to dissipate static)
Lightweight thermoelectric power sources (e.g., Soviet Lunokhod rovers used 210Po heaters)
Neutron sources by mixing 210Po with beryllium (Po-Be sources)
Brushes for removing dust from photographic film and lenses (largely obsolete)
Radioisotope thermal heaters for spacecraft components (historical use)
Initiators in some early nuclear weapon designs (historical use, now obsolete)
reactivity_summary: Polonium is a silvery, volatile, intensely radioactive metalloid/post-transition metal whose chemistry resembles that of tellurium and bismuth. The +4 state is most common, but +2, +6, and -2 are also known. It dissolves readily in dilute acids to form pink Po(II) or yellow Po(IV) solutions but only slowly in alkalis. The radioactivity (mostly alpha) produces tremendous self-heating: a milligram of 210Po glows blue from its radioluminescence. Polonium is one of the most toxic substances known by mass, on the order of a microgram being a lethal dose if ingested.
isotopes_summary: Polonium has no stable isotopes; 33 isotopes are known, all radioactive. The longest-lived is 209Po (t1/2 ~125 yr), followed by 208Po (t1/2 ~2.9 yr). The most familiar is 210Po (t1/2 138.4 d), a near-pure alpha emitter used in industrial and research applications and infamously linked to the 2006 poisoning of Alexander Litvinenko. Several short-lived isotopes (212Po, 214Po, 218Po) appear in the natural U-238 and Th-232 decay chains.
